title: REPAIR Act of 2025
plain_summary: The REPAIR Act of 2025 makes new rules for when people want to challenge government permits for big projects in court. It gives people only a short amount of time to file lawsuits about these permits and limits who can sue. If a court finds problems with a permit, it must send it back to the government agency to fix instead of stopping the whole project. The bill also creates a way for people to work out disagreements through mediation instead of going to court. Projects can only be stopped completely if they would cause immediate danger to people.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Revising and Enhancing Project Authorizations Impacted by Review Act of two thousand twenty five or the REPAIR Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these words have these meanings. Agency means any federal government department or office that has power to make decisions. Agency of jurisdiction means any agency that has the power to approve a permit or license under federal laws. Authorization means any license, permit, approval, or other official decision that federal law requires or allows before someone can design, plan, build, change, or operate a project. This includes any extensions of these permits or approvals. Authorizing legislation means specific federal laws that control the environment and natural resources. These laws include the Clean Air Act, the Federal Water Pollution Control Act, the Natural Gas Act, the Federal Power Act, the National Historic Preservation Act, the Endangered Species Act, the Migratory Bird Treaty Act, the Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act, the Marine Mammal Protection Act, the Coastal Zone Management Act, the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act, the Mineral Leasing Act, the Safe Drinking Water Act for certain state programs, the Deepwater Port Act, the Atomic Energy Act, the Geothermal Steam Act, the National Forest Management Act, the Forest and Rangeland Renewable Resources Planning Act, the Federal Land Policy and Management Act, the Solid Waste Disposal Act, the Comprehensive Environmental Response Compensation and Liability Act, the Urban Park and Recreation Recovery Act, and any other federal law that requires an environmental review under the National Environmental Policy Act. Council means the Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council that was created by the FAST Act. Direct and tangible harm means harm that is directly connected to a project and causes either physical illness or injury to a person or economic loss that is not paid back to the person who suffered the loss. Environmental review means an assessment of how a project might affect the environment that is prepared under the National Environmental Policy Act. This review leads to documents like an environmental assessment, a finding of no significant impact, an environmental impact statement, a record of decision, or other reviews required by that law. Project means an activity that needs to get an authorization under the authorizing legislation listed above. Project sponsor means the agency or other group, including private companies or public private partnerships, that asks the agency of jurisdiction for approval of a project.

sections.title: Section 3 Judicial Review
sections.text: This section sets time limits and rules for court challenges to project permits. People who want to challenge the initial approval process or the permit itself in court must file their case within one hundred twenty days after the final agency decision is made, unless federal law sets a shorter time limit. Any additional court actions related to that first challenge, including requests for court orders to stop the project, must be filed within one hundred twenty days after the first challenge was filed. If someone fails to file these additional actions by the deadline, their original challenge becomes invalid and they lose the right to bring that additional claim. Other types of challenges to permits must follow the mediation process described later in this section. When a court finds that an agency did not follow the law when granting a permit, the default solution is to send the permit back to the agency to fix the problems. A court can only stop or cancel a permit if allowing the project would create immediate and serious danger to human health or the environment and no other fair legal remedy exists. Only people who will suffer direct and tangible harm from a project can challenge its permit in court, and only if that harm was not studied when the original permit was approved. If a court stops, sends back, or cancels a project permit, the project sponsor and the agency must work together in a mediation process overseen by the Council to fix the problems and get the project reapproved. Within sixty days after a court stops or cancels a permit, both the project sponsor and the agency must submit plans to the Council explaining how to fix the identified problems or reduce their impact if they cannot be fully fixed. A project sponsor can ask for up to one hundred twenty more days to complete their plan, and the Council must approve this request. If the agency fails to submit a plan on time, the Council must approve the project sponsor's plan and tell the agency to reapprove all permits for the project. Within sixty days after both parties submit their plans, the Council must hold meetings between them, create a final fix plan, and tell the agency to reapprove the project based on that plan. The final plan must contain changes needed to address why the court stopped the project. The plan will either accept the project sponsor's proposal or change it based on the agency's proposal, trying to find a middle ground between them. The final plan cannot be longer than fifty pages. At any time during the sixty day period, either party can accept the other party's proposal, and the Council will make that the final plan and tell the agency to reapprove all permits. The Council must hold at least one meeting between the parties to resolve disagreements. When the final plan is complete, the Council must make it public and tell the agency to reapprove all permits. If the Council fails to direct reapproval within sixty days, the agency must consider the project sponsor's plan as the final plan and reapprove all permits. The Council can hold meetings between the parties before they submit their plans to help them agree on solutions. Plans should use existing information from the original permit process and court case whenever possible. If additional analysis is needed to fulfill a court order, the final plan must choose one agency to do the analysis, allow no more than ninety days for the analysis, consider the project reapproved if the analysis is not completed in ninety days, and establish clear actions based on the analysis results. No one except the project sponsor can challenge the final plan in court. The agency must reapprove all permits within fifteen days after the final plan is completed. If the agency fails to do this, the project sponsor can begin work that depends on having the permits. Court challenges to project permits must be filed in the court that covers the area where the project is located, or if the project crosses multiple court areas, in the court that covers the area where the most money will be invested in the project. Courts must randomly assign these cases to judges to avoid any appearance of favoritism or bias. The Council must keep a public database of permit challenges that have not been decided within ninety days after being assigned to a judge. The Director of the Administrative Office of US Courts must report to the Council about cases that take longer than ninety days, including the case name, the law involved, the parties' names, the filing date, the court name, and the judge's name. People involved in cases can also report this information themselves. The Council must update the database daily to show how long each case has been under review and must publish the reported information within five business days. At least once per year, the Council must publish a report listing all late cases, courts with multiple late cases, judges with multiple late cases, any judges who failed to report properly, and other information needed to ensure timely case review. The report must be published in the Federal Register and on the Council's website and sent to specific congressional committees. These rules apply to projects that were submitted for approval before this law was passed and to any court challenges that were already in progress when this law was passed. This section does not create new rights to sue under environmental laws that do not already allow lawsuits, and it does not prevent project sponsors from appealing agency rejections or resubmitting projects to address rejection reasons.

sections.title: Section 4 Judicial Standing Under NEPA
sections.text: The National Environmental Policy Act is changed by adding a new section. The new section says that nothing in the National Environmental Policy Act or any environmental review done under that law gives anyone the right to sue in court under that law or the Administrative Procedure Act about the approval of a project permit that used an environmental review.
